entry.title: IOS granular permissions
entry.content:
Preamble

If you have tiered levels of administrators, or you want to
create an account for automation purposes best practice is to
define a custom security level in IOS.

Levels 1 and 15 are defined by default.

Allowing lower levels to see your configuration: IOS

conf t
username backup privilege 3 secret <SECRET>
privilege exec level 3 show startup-config
privilege exec level 3 show
wr mem

Allowing lower levels to see your configuration: FreeRADIUS:

/etc/freeradius/users


backup
    Service-Type = NAS-Prompt-User,
    cisco-avpair = "shell:priv-lvl=3",
    Auth-Type = System,

Looks Like


cisco#show start
Building configuration...

--CONFIGURATION--
end

Defining the next user tier for NAT information: IOS

conf t
username natinfo privilege 4 secret <SECRET>
privilege exec level 4 show nat64 statistics
wr mem

Defining the next user tier for NAT information: FreeRADIUS


natinfo
    Service-Type = NAS-Prompt-User,
    cisco-avpair = "shell:priv-lvl=4",
    Auth-Type = System,

Looks Like

cisco#show ip nat statistics
--STATS--

NOTES

  • Permissions stack up which means a user at Level 4 can also issue 'show startup-configuration'
  • 'show running-config' command does not work in this way for the IOS devices I have tried.

This does not work how you think it does:

privilege exec level 3 show running-config

Reference

http://www.techrepublic.com/article/understand-the-levels-of-privilege-in-the-cisco-ios/
http://www.cisco.com/en/US/docs/ios/12_2/security/configuration/guide/scfpass.html

entry.title: proxychains
entry.content:
Preamble

Some commands do not support a proxy (RE: telnet). Other
times it is just easier to do a one-off instance rather
than mess with environment settings. A lot of people use
this with Tor.

ProxyChains

Allows you to send traffic through a proxy without the command
itself having foreknowledge.

aptitude install proxychains

Config files

Pick one:

  • ./proxychains.conf
  • $(HOME)/.proxychains/proxychains.conf
  • /etc/proxychains.conf

/etc/proxychains.conf the global option


strict_chain
quiet_mode
tcp_read_time_out 15000
tcp_connect_time_out 8000
[ProxyList]
#replace this with your proxy
http    192.168.1.1 8080

telnet Before Proxychains (behind a proxy)


me@vm:~/pkg$ telnet google.com 80
Trying 2404:6800:4005:806::1006...
Trying 173.194.127.196...
Trying 173.194.127.193...
Trying 173.194.127.200...
Trying 173.194.127.201...
Trying 173.194.127.198...
Trying 173.194.127.199...
Trying 173.194.127.195...
Trying 173.194.127.192...
Trying 173.194.127.206...
Trying 173.194.127.194...
Trying 173.194.127.197...
telnet: Unable to connect to remote host: Network is unreachable

telnet after ProxyChains (behind a proxy)


me@vm:~/pkg$ proxychains telnet google.com 80
ProxyChains-3.1 (http://proxychains.sf.net)
Trying 2404:6800:4005:806::1006...
Trying 173.194.127.196...
Connected to google.com.
Escape character is '^]'.

Excluding traffic from your proxy

In theory you set the localnet option in your configuration file
but this doesn't work me with proxychains under debian.

Just in case you are interested though: localnet docs

Reference

http://benizi.com/postfix-proxychains
http://www.jameslovecomputers.com/how-to-install-configure-and-use-proxychains/
http://proxychains.sourceforge.net/howto.html
http://proxychains.sourceforge.net/howto.html
https://github.com/haad/proxychains

entry.title: allowing env magic via sudo
entry.content:
Preamble

In an environment with heavy automation and numerous service account
users it is easy to end up in a situation where compromising one service
becomes compromising an entire system.

Granting permission to a single command

vi /etc/sudoers.d/makingpie

myuser ALL = NOPASSWD: /usr/local/bin/makepie

Looks Like

myuser:#sudo makepie
>>>>mmmmm...pie

What if my user needs to call out to a proxy but 'makepie' doesn't support it?

We use proxychains.

sudo LD_PRELOAD=libproxychains.so.3 makepie

Sudo is extra granular and doesn't like this idea.

sudo: sorry, you are not allowed to set the following environment variables: LD_PRELOAD

The heavy handed way

Don't reset user enviroments when using sudo

visudo

Replace: “Defaults env_reset” with: “Defaults !env_reset”

Piecemeal way

vi /etc/sudoers.d/ld_preload

#allow users to use proxychains env variable (using sudo)
Defaults env_keep += "LD_PRELOAD"

Reference

http://brandonhutchinson.com/wiki/Sudo_and_environment_variables
http://unixhelp.ed.ac.uk/CGI/man-cgi?sudoers+5
http://askubuntu.com/questions/57915/environment-variables-when-run-with-sudo
http://webonrails.com/2012/07/02/allowing-user-to-set-environment-variable-with-sudo/
http://superuser.com/questions/232231/how-do-i-make-sudo-preserve-my-environment-variables
http://askubuntu.com/questions/128413/setting-the-path-so-it-applies-to-all-users-including-root-sudo
http://www.ibm.com/developerworks/aix/library/au-sudo/

entry.title: making trivial debian packages
entry.content:
Preamble

When possible it is beneficial having a canonical reference for resources installed
on a server. Binary packages have built in logic for upgrade, downgrade,
install, etc. This makes them an attractive, yet sometimes, overcomplicated
distribution mechanisms.

I am going to demonstrate making a simple script into a debian binary
and installing it. Adding this to an in-house repo would be a nice next
step.

Getting Packages

Assuming you have dpkg but if not we need it. Fakeroot we use to make packages
with the right permissions on files.

aptitude install dpkg fakeroot lintian

We will be using dpkg-deb which is generally considered 'built in' to debian.


me@vm:# dpkg -S /usr/bin/dpkg-deb
dpkg: /usr/bin/dpkg-debø

Package: my-files.

Starting at version 1.0 and meant for all architectures. 'all' is a common
assignment for architecture when the files/scripts are interpreted such as
with python or ruby.

mkdir my-files_1.0_all

Making my package tree

mkdir -p my-files_1.0_all/usr/local/bin/
mkdir -p my-files_1.0_all/DEBIAN

My script: aptitude easter eggs

nano my-files_1.0_all/usr/local/bin/moo


#!/bin/bash
aptitude -v -v -v -v -v moo

Adding my 'control' file

#this file contains my package metadata
nano my-files_1.0_all/DEBIAN/control

Looks Like


Package: my-files
Priority: optional
Section: misc
Maintainer: Chase 
Architecture: all
Version: 1.0
Description: stuff I need

Making my script executable on install

#postint will be run at the tail end of install by default
echo "chmod +x /usr/local/bin/moo" > my-files_1.0_all/DEBIAN/postinst
#set correct permissions to avoid build errors
chmod 555 my-files_1.0_all/DEBIAN/postinst

My full package tree


my-files_1.0_all
|-- DEBIAN
|   |-- control
|   `-- postinst
`-- usr
    `-- local
        `-- bin
            `-- moo

Building my binary

Fakeroot should fix up your permissions, permissions are taken very literally by
dpkg-deb. So if you build with a user that does not exist on a host that installs
this package things break. But even if you don't run into that it is considered
bad form to leave your user permissions as artifacts on binary packaged files.

fakeroot dpkg-deb -b my-files_1.0_all/

Looks Like

dpkg-deb: building package `my-files' in `my-files_1.0_all.deb'.

Installing and Examining my package


me@vm:# dpkg -i my-files_1.0_all.deb 
Selecting previously unselected package my-files.
(Reading database ... 49666 files and directories currently installed.)
Unpacking my-files (from my-files_1.0_all.deb) ...
Setting up my-files (1.0) ...
me@vm:# 

me@vm:# dpkg -L my-files             
/.
/usr
/usr/local
/usr/local/bin
/usr/local/bin/moo

me@vm:~/pkg# dpkg -s my-files
Package: my-files
Status: install ok installed
Priority: optional
Section: misc
Maintainer: Chase 
Architecture: all
Version: 1.0
Description: stuff I need

Using Lintian

Lintian breaks down deb binaries and examines them. If we are making a package for
repo distribution or contribution upstream in some manner we want this output to look
at nice as it can. For this package I am just making sure we understanding what
compromises we are making.


me@vm:~/pkg$ lintian my-files_1.0_all.deb 
E: my-files: control-file-has-bad-permissions postinst 0555 != 0755
E: my-files: no-copyright-file
E: my-files: extended-description-is-empty
E: my-files: dir-in-usr-local usr/local/bin/
E: my-files: file-in-usr-local usr/local/bin/moo
W: my-files: file-in-unusual-dir usr/local/bin/moo
W: my-files: script-not-executable usr/local/bin/moo

Running my command


me@vm:# moo
All right, you win.

                               /----\
                       -------/      \
                      /               \
                     /                |
   -----------------/                  --------\
   ----------------------------------------------

Reference

http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/hardy/man1/dpkg-deb.1.html
http://askubuntu.com/questions/62534/adding-post-deb-install-scripts
http://www.debian.org/doc/devel-manuals#maint-guide
http://www.debian.org/doc/manuals/debian-faq/ch-pkg_basics.en.html
http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/30303/how-to-create-a-deb-file-manually
http://ailoo.net/2009/06/repack-a-deb-archive-with-dpkg-deb/
http://tldp.org/HOWTO/html_single/Debian-Binary-Package-Building-HOWTO/#AEN108

entry.title: simple nat outbound asa
entry.content:
Preamble

NAT on the ASA can get complicated but 90% of remote sites need
some version of this basic configuration.

Interfaces

Establish your NAT roles.


!
interface Ethernet0/0
 nameif Outside
 security-level 0
 ip address  
!
interface Ethernet0/1
 nameif Inside
 security-level 100
 ip address

Client Pool

Define who gets an address for going out.


object network NAT-CLIENTS
 !can be a subnet also, etc
 range 192.168.1.1 192.168.1.10

NAT Enable

Associate your clients with your external interface for NAT.


nat (Inside,Outside) source dynamic NAT-CLIENTS interface

NOTES

  • This does not require extra global IP's.
  • This dynamic IP may supercede intended static NAT IP's
  • Firewall restrictions still apply. See docs on security levels.

Reference

https://sso.cisco.com/autho/forms/CDClogin.html#wp1118157

entry.title: making debs with rake
entry.content:
Preamble

Some Rake files have built in logic to make a debian binary.

Mcollective

A good example:

git clone https://github.com/puppetlabs/marionette-collective
cd marionette-collective/

Getting Packages

aptitude install rake \
build-essential 
rubygem \
pdebuild-cross \
devscripts \
fakeroot \
dpatch

Making Debian Package

rake deb

Looks Like


rm -r build
rm -r doc
Parsing sources...



dpkg-deb: building package `mcollective' in `../mcollective_2.3.2-1_all.deb'.
dh_gencontrol -pmcollective-client  
dpkg-gencontrol: warning: File::FcntlLock not available; using flock which is not NFS-safe
dh_md5sums -pmcollective-client 
dh_builddeb -pmcollective-client 
dpkg-deb: building package `mcollective-client' in `../mcollective-client_2.3.2-1_all.deb'.
dh_gencontrol -pmcollective-common  
dpkg-gencontrol: warning: File::FcntlLock not available; using flock which is not NFS-safe
dh_md5sums -pmcollective-common 
dh_builddeb -pmcollective-common 
dpkg-deb: building package `mcollective-common' in `../mcollective-common_2.3.2-1_all.deb'.
dh_gencontrol -pmcollective-doc  
dpkg-gencontrol: warning: File::FcntlLock not available; using flock which is not NFS-safe
dh_md5sums -pmcollective-doc 
dh_builddeb -pmcollective-doc 
dpkg-deb: building package `mcollective-doc' in `../mcollective-doc_2.3.2-1_all.deb'.
 dpkg-genchanges  >../mcollective_2.3.2-1_amd64.changes
dpkg-genchanges: including full source code in upload
 dpkg-source -i --after-build mcollective-2.3.2
dpkg-buildpackage: full upload (original source is included)

Normal install

dpkg -i mcollective_2.3.2-1_all.deb

entry.title: mcollective bugs debian wheezy
entry.content:
Preamble

Debian Wheezy is on mcollective 2.0. There are several agents that require
at least 2.2. You can download from the official PuppetLabs repository version
but for Wheezy there are several bugs.

Dowloading official Package

Do this:

http://docs.puppetlabs.com/guides/puppetlabs_package_repositories.html

wget http://apt-enterprise.puppetlabs.com/puppetlabs-enterprise-release-extras_1.0-2_all.deb
sudo dpkg -i puppetlabs-enterprise-release-extras_1.0-2_all.deb
sudo apt-get update
aptitude install mcollective

You will notice it doesn't work. Suck. Ok let's build our own.

Bug #1 fails to run on debian/ubuntu with ruby 1.9

The system default for ruby is 1.9. Mcollective has ruby 1.8 paths hardcoded in several places, but
the main binary uses the environment settings. This mismatch causes issues.

Bug demonstration

/etc/init.d/mcollective restart


/usr/lib/ruby/1.9.1/rubygems/custom_require.rb:36:in `require': cannot load such file -- mcollective (LoadError)
        from /usr/lib/ruby/1.9.1/rubygems/custom_require.rb:36:in `require'
    from /usr/sbin/mcollectived:3:in `
'

mcollective puts files under ruby 1.8

dpkg -L mcollective-common


/usr/lib/ruby/1.8/mcollective
/usr/lib/ruby/1.8/mcollective/logger
/usr/lib/ruby/1.8/mcollective/logger/syslog_logger.rb
/usr/lib/ruby/1.8/mcollective/logger/base.rb
/usr/lib/ruby/1.8/mcollective/logger/console_logger.rb
/usr/lib/ruby/1.8/mcollective/logger/file_logger.rb

But Wheezy uses 1.9

ruby 1.9.3p194 (2012-04-20 revision 35410) [x86_64-linux]

Simple Fix

vi bin/mcollectived

This:

#!/usr/bin/env ruby

Becomes:

#!/usr/bin/ruby1.8

Bug 2: ubuntu/debian mcollective missing package dependencies

Depencies are not complete for Debian.

Looks Like

/usr/lib/ruby/vendor_ruby/1.8/rubygems/custom_require.rb:36:in `gem_original_require': no such file to load - -- stomp

Simple Fix

sudo apt-get install ruby-stomp

Stomp Connector Not Included

This is not my code, it is from somewhere in the mcollective world. Including here
for completeness.

mcollective/connectors/stomp.rb


require 'stomp'

module MCollective
  module Connector
    # Handles sending and receiving messages over the Stomp protocol
    #
    # This plugin supports version 1.1 or 1.1.6 and newer of the Stomp rubygem
    # the versions between those had multi threading issues.
    #
    # For all versions you can configure it as follows:
    #
    #    connector = stomp
    #    plugin.stomp.host = stomp.your.net
    #    plugin.stomp.port = 6163
    #    plugin.stomp.user = you
    #    plugin.stomp.password = secret
    #
    # All of these can be overriden per user using environment variables:
    #
    #    STOMP_SERVER, STOMP_PORT, STOMP_USER, STOMP_PASSWORD
    #
    # Version 1.1.6 onward support supplying multiple connections and it will
    # do failover between these servers, you can configure it as follows:
    #
    #     connector = stomp
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.size = 2
    #
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.host1 = stomp1.your.net
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.port1 = 6163
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.user1 = you
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.password1 = secret
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.ssl1 = true
    #
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.host2 = stomp2.your.net
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.port2 = 6163
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.user2 = you
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.password2 = secret
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.ssl2 = false
    #
    # Using this method you can supply just STOMP_USER and STOMP_PASSWORD
    # you have to supply the hostname for each pool member in the config.
    # The port will default to 6163 if not specified.
    #
    # In addition you can set the following options but only when using
    # pooled configuration:
    #
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.initial_reconnect_delay = 0.01
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.max_reconnect_delay = 30.0
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.use_exponential_back_off = true
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.back_off_multiplier = 2
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.max_reconnect_attempts = 0
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.randomize = false
    #     plugin.stomp.pool.timeout = -1
    #
    # For versions of ActiveMQ that supports message priorities
    # you can set a priority, this will cause a "priority" header
    # to be emitted if present:
    #
    #     plugin.stomp.priority = 4
    #
    class Stomp hosts}

            # Various STOMP gem options, defaults here matches defaults for 1.1.6 the meaning of
            # these can be guessed, the documentation isn't clear
            connection[:initial_reconnect_delay] = get_option("stomp.pool.initial_reconnect_delay", 0.01).to_f
            connection[:max_reconnect_delay] = get_option("stomp.pool.max_reconnect_delay", 30.0).to_f
            connection[:use_exponential_back_off] = get_bool_option("stomp.pool.use_exponential_back_off", true)
            connection[:back_off_multiplier] = get_bool_option("stomp.pool.back_off_multiplier", 2).to_i
            connection[:max_reconnect_attempts] = get_option("stomp.pool.max_reconnect_attempts", 0).to_i
            connection[:randomize] = get_bool_option("stomp.pool.randomize", false)
            connection[:backup] = get_bool_option("stomp.pool.backup", false)
            connection[:timeout] = get_option("stomp.pool.timeout", -1).to_i
            connection[:reliable] = true

            connection[:logger] = EventLogger.new

            @connection = connector.new(connection)
          end
        rescue Exception => e
          raise("Could not connect to Stomp Server: #{e}")
        end
      end

      # Receives a message from the Stomp connection
      def receive
        Log.debug("Waiting for a message from Stomp")

        # When the Stomp library > 1.2.0 is mid reconnecting due to its reliable connection
        # handling it sets the connection to closed.  If we happen to be receiving at just
        # that time we will get an exception warning about the closed connection so handling
        # that here with a sleep and a retry.
        begin
          msg = @connection.receive
        rescue ::Stomp::Error::NoCurrentConnection
          sleep 1
          retry
        end

        Message.new(msg.body, msg, :base64 => @base64, :headers => msg.headers)
      end

      # Sends a message to the Stomp connection
      def publish(msg)
        msg.base64_encode! if @base64

        raise "Cannot set specific reply to targets with the STOMP plugin" if msg.reply_to

        if msg.type == :direct_request
          msg.discovered_hosts.each do |node|
            target = make_target(msg.agent, msg.type, msg.collective, node)

            Log.debug("Sending a direct message to STOMP target '#{target}'")

            publish_msg(target, msg.payload)
          end
        else
          target = make_target(msg.agent, msg.type, msg.collective)

          Log.debug("Sending a broadcast message to STOMP target '#{target}'")

          publish_msg(target, msg.payload)
        end
      end

      # Subscribe to a topic or queue
      def subscribe(agent, type, collective)
        source = make_target(agent, type, collective)

        unless @subscriptions.include?(source)
          Log.debug("Subscribing to #{source}")
          @connection.subscribe(source)
          @subscriptions << source
        end
      rescue ::Stomp::Error::DuplicateSubscription
        Log.debug("Received subscription for #{source[:name]} but already had a subscription, ignoring")
      end

      # Actually sends the message to the middleware
      def publish_msg(target, msg)
        # deal with deprecation warnings in newer stomp gems
        if @connection.respond_to?("publish")
          @connection.publish(target, msg, msgheaders)
        else
          @connection.send(target, msg, msgheaders)
        end
      end

      # Subscribe to a topic or queue
      def unsubscribe(agent, type, collective)
        source = make_target(agent, type, collective)

        Log.debug("Unsubscribing from #{source}")
        @connection.unsubscribe(source)
        @subscriptions.delete(source)
      end

      # Disconnects from the Stomp connection
      def disconnect
        Log.debug("Disconnecting from Stomp")
        @connection.disconnect
      end

      def msgheaders
        headers = {}
        headers = {"priority" => @msgpriority} if @msgpriority > 0

        return headers
      end

      # looks in the environment first then in the config file
      # for a specific option, accepts an optional default.
      #
      # raises an exception when it cant find a value anywhere
      def get_env_or_option(env, opt, default=nil)
        return ENV[env] if ENV.include?(env)
        return @config.pluginconf[opt] if @config.pluginconf.include?(opt)
        return default if default

        raise("No #{env} environment or plugin.#{opt} configuration option given")
      end

      # looks for a config option, accepts an optional default
      #
      # raises an exception when it cant find a value anywhere
      def get_option(opt, default=nil)
        return @config.pluginconf[opt] if @config.pluginconf.include?(opt)
        return default if default

        raise("No plugin.#{opt} configuration option given")
      end

      # gets a boolean option from the config, supports y/n/true/false/1/0
      def get_bool_option(opt, default)
        return default unless @config.pluginconf.include?(opt)

        val = @config.pluginconf[opt]

        if val =~ /^1|yes|true/
          return true
        elsif val =~ /^0|no|false/
          return false
        else
          return default
        end
      end

      def make_target(agent, type, collective, target_node=nil)
        raise("Unknown target type #{type}") unless [:directed, :broadcast, :reply, :request, :direct_request].include?(type)
        raise("Unknown collective '#{collective}' known collectives are '#{@config.collectives.join ', '}'") unless @config.collectives.include?(collective)

        prefix = @config.topicprefix

        case type
          when :reply
            suffix = :reply
          when :broadcast
            suffix = :command
          when :request
            suffix = :command
          when :direct_request
            agent = nil
            prefix = @config.queueprefix
            suffix = Digest::MD5.hexdigest(target_node)
          when :directed
            agent = nil
            prefix = @config.queueprefix
            # use a md5 since hostnames might have illegal characters that
            # the middleware dont understand
            suffix = Digest::MD5.hexdigest(@config.identity)
        end

        ["#{prefix}#{collective}", agent, suffix].compact.join(@config.topicsep)
      end
    end
  end
end

Reference

http://askubuntu.com/questions/15211/saving-deb-files-from-repositories-to-a-custom-location-for-installing-offline
http://projects.puppetlabs.com/issues/17804
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/mcollective-users/5o8QT_myk0M
http://serverfault.com/questions/386646/mcollective-cannot-load-the-stomp-library
http://anonscm.debian.org/gitweb/?p=pkg-puppet/mcollective.git;a=blob;f=debian/mcollective-common.install;h=829ef98bbed0822f0bdb7fdcd755403b72b4563f;hb=d4d5f2573d82fd565e0c6b460677e05000e53cda
https://github.com/puppetlabs/marionette-collective/tree/master/spec
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/puppet-users/oYLo9VuZtTk/OCwyAWz-xc4J

entry.title: memcollective tricks
entry.content:
Preamble

mcollective-isms.

Find all hosts

/usr/local/bin/mc-find-hosts


#!/bin/bash
if [ "$1" == "" ]; then
    /usr/bin/mco find --wc mcollective
else
    /usr/bin/mco find $@


mc-find-hosts

Find host by name matching

mc-find-hosts --with-id /foo/

Find hosts by puppet class

/usr/sbin/mc-find-hosts --with-class <class_name> | sort

Query service status by class

mco service --with-class /apache/ apache2 status

Getting stats about the service itself

mco rpc rpcutil daemon_stats

Restarting a service

mco service --with-identity <host> <service> restart

Find agents directory

sudo find / -name rpcutil.ddl -exec dirname {} \;

Query a fact

mco facts <fact_name>

Query Puppet Status and get JSON in return

mco rpc puppet status -j

entry.title: nix lights out failsafe
entry.content:
Preamble

Just like for cisco a
scheduled reboot can be an aggressive but necessary recovery option. If you are doing
iptables maintenance remote I always like to store my rules in a script and run them
without saving. With a scheduled reboot pending locking myself out is a recoverable
offense.

/usr/local/bin/rsched


#!/bin/bash
if [ -z "$1" ]
  then
    echo "Usage: $0 "
    exit 1
fi
echo "reboot interval $1 minutes"
now=`date +"%s"`
#convert seconds to minutes
future_time=`expr $1 \\* 60`
rtime=`expr $now + $future_time`
#convert epoch to shutdown friendly format
time=`date -d @$rtime +"%H:%M"`
echo $time
#issue shutdown
shutdown -k -r $time

Looks like

root@vm:~# rsched 10
reboot interval 10 minutes
14:11

The system is going DOWN for reboot in 10 minutes!/0) (Wed Jul 10 14:01:57 20

Scheduling

root@vm:~# nohup rsched 10 &

Broadcast message from root@vm (Wed Jul 10 14:03:02 2013):

The system is going DOWN for reboot in 10 minutes!

do my fw maint

Canceling

root@vm:~# fg
nohup rsched 10

<cntrl+c>

Notes

redirect bg process with &> /tmp/reboot

entry.title: cli password generation
entry.content:
Preamble

I have seen usage of http://www.pctools.com/guides/password/ and similar sites. This
is less than ideal. APG is my generator of choice.

Six twelve character choices

root@vm:~# apg -m 12
recEbrArkyan
eevocdeirpUd
tejvivFedAt*
loolNuag>olk
ejfoctOdofmu
pidChiemp2of

Six twelve character choices with phonetics

root@vm:~# apg -m 12 -t
daHedVuobgis (da-Hed-Vu-ob-gis)
lokhanKujTue (lok-han-Kuj-Tue)
ebviHeetsos$ (eb-vi-Heets-os-DOLLAR_SIGN)
agborjotnans (ag-borj-ot-nans)
RutElAmJomt8 (Rut-El-Am-Jomt-EIGHT)
DeOsacFerhyu (De-Os-ac-Fer-hyu)

One twelve character password

root@vm:~# apg -m 12 -t -n 1
JetHikAgVup1 (Jet-Hik-Ag-Vup-ONE)

One twelve character password with readback

root@vm:~# apg -m 12 -t -n 1 -l
yoawhathuct3 (yoaw-hat-huct-THREE) yankee-oscar-alfa-whiskey-hotel-alfa-tango-hotel-uniform-charlie-tango-THREE

Increasing randomness with manual entry

root@vm:~# apg -m 12 -t -n 1 -l -s
Please enter some random data (only first 16 are significant)
(eg. your old password):>
yufNifEyLyff (yuf-Nif-Ey-Lyff) yankee-uniform-foxtrot-November-india-foxtrot-Echo-yankee-Lima-yankee-foxtrot-foxtrot

Reference

http://www.pctools.com/guides/password/
http://www.linux-magazine.com/Online/Features/Testing-Password-Quality
https://help.ubuntu.com/community/StrongPasswords#Installing%20APG

entry.title: virsh halting a host
entry.content:
Preamble

KVM is great but I'm aking a note so I remember because this command gives me pause every time.

Sometimes 'shutdown' in the virtual shell for KVM fails.

virsh # shutdown myvm
Domain myvm is being shutdown

But...

114 myvm            running

In order to shutdown this host I use the 'destroy' command. Destroy does not
remove any files or otherwise permanently change the host data. Which for me,
is unintuitive. It does remove it from the list of hosts shown with the 'list'
command. Destroy is like pulling the plug.

virsh #destroy myvm

The command that does what I would expect destroy to do is: 'undefine'.

Reference
http://docs.fedoraproject.org/en-US/Fedora/13/html/Virtualization_Guide/chap-Virtualization-Managing_guests_with_virsh.html
http://www.cyberciti.biz/faq/linux-kvm-stop-start-guest-virtual-machine/

entry.title: mysql cleanup
entry.content:
Show me my non-default databases:

for d in $(mysql -u root -e "show databases" | \
grep -v 'Database\|mysql\|performance_schema\|information_schema'); \
do echo $d; done

Clean non-default databases:

for d in $(mysql -u root -e "show databases" | \
grep -v 'Database\|mysql\|performance_schema\|information_schema'); \
do echo $d; mysql -u root -e "drop database $d;"; done

entry.title: puppetmaster_3 on debian
entry.content:
Preamble

Getting puppetmaster 3.0 on Debian (still squeeze)

I am trying to test and I keep seeing:

Syntax error at '('; expected ')' at /repo/modules/apache/manifests/module.pp:34 on node test

I have seen this before and I know it is a result of manifests written for >3.0, at least for this

specific issue. That error is rather generic in general.

Can I use backports?

aptitude update
aptitude -t squeeze-backports install puppetmaster

--> puppet: Depends: puppet-common (= 2.6.2-5+squeeze6) but 2.6.2-5+squeeze8 is to be installed.

No, backports is still pretty old.

Using the Official Repo

Luckily, PuppetEnterprises publishes a deb for adding their repo. Similar to what rpmforge does for any
CentOS folks.

wget http://apt.puppetlabs.com/puppetlabs-release-sqeeze.deb

--> 2013-06-27 16:11:21 (159 MB/s) - “puppetlabs-release-squeeze.deb” saved [3440/3440]

dpkg -i puppetlabs-release-squeeze.deb 
cat /etc/apt/sources.list.d/puppetlabs.list


# Puppetlabs products
deb http://apt.puppetlabs.com squeeze main
deb-src http://apt.puppetlabs.com squeeze main

# Puppetlabs dependencies
deb http://apt.puppetlabs.com squeeze dependencies
deb-src http://apt.puppetlabs.com squeeze dependencies

# Puppetlabs devel (uncomment to activate)
# deb http://apt.puppetlabs.com squeeze devel
# deb-src http://apt.puppetlabs.com squeeze devel

sudo aptitude update
apt-cache show puppetmaster

--> Package: puppetmaster
--> Version: 2.6.2-5+squeeze6


aptitude install puppetmaster

--> Package: puppetmaster
--> ...
--> Version: 3.2.2-1puppetlabs1

Reference:

http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/39261/how-to-see-package-version-without-install
http://docs.puppetlabs.com/guides/puppetlabs#### package#### repositories.html
http://packages.debian.org/search?keywords=puppetmaster&searchon=names&section=all&suite=squeeze-backports
http://backports.debian.org/Instructions/

entry.title: simple site loadtesting
entry.content:
Preamble

Throwing some load at your setup and seeing how it reacts.

There is a difference between problem solving, and solving problems at scale.

Get some tools

On your user host:

aptitude install httping seige apache2 bc

On your webserver:

aptitude install htop

Seige is a lowkey load test tool, httping is nice for measuring response times in a
consistent way while load testing, and apache is because we want the apache benchmark
tool or 'ab'.

root@<lhost>:~# which ab
/usr/bin/ab

Launching a test with seige: 3 concurrent users with 3 connections

~# siege -b -c 3 -r 3 <webserver>:80


** SIEGE 2.70
** Preparing 3 concurrent users for battle.
The server is now under siege...
HTTP/1.1 200   0.01 secs:     146 bytes ==> /
HTTP/1.1 200   0.01 secs:     146 bytes ==> /
HTTP/1.1 200   0.01 secs:     146 bytes ==> /
HTTP/1.1 200   0.00 secs:     146 bytes ==> /
HTTP/1.1 200   0.00 secs:     146 bytes ==> /
HTTP/1.1 200   0.00 secs:     146 bytes ==> /
HTTP/1.1 200   0.00 secs:     146 bytes ==> /
HTTP/1.1 200   0.00 secs:     146 bytes ==> /
HTTP/1.1 200   0.00 secs:     146 bytes ==> /
done.
Transactions:                  9 hits
Availability:             100.00 %
Elapsed time:               0.01 secs
Data transferred:           0.00 MB
Response time:              0.00 secs
Transaction rate:         900.00 trans/sec
Throughput:             0.13 MB/sec
Concurrency:                3.00
Successful transactions:           9
Failed transactions:               0
Longest transaction:            0.01
Shortest transaction:           0.00

This is not a great indicator of performance under load.

When I raise it to 3000 users at 10 requests I start seeing:

descriptor table full sock.c:108: Too many open files

So I raise the descriptor limit.

cat /proc/sys/fs/file-max
1645928

You can see your limits using:

ulimit -Sn
ulimit -Hn

Now I see too much of:

socket: connection timed out
[error] socket: unable to connect sock.c:222: Operation already in progress

So I'm not too happy with seige. For some tests it seems ok. For large test pools it hasn't
won me over. Then again the launching host isn't that great so maybe it's on my side.

A good compromise I found was:

siege -b -c 1000 -r 3 <webserver>:80

Trying ab...

A thousand concurrent threads with a thousand requests.

ab -n 1000 -c 1000 http://<webserver>/


This is ApacheBench, Version 2.3 <$Revision: 655654 $>
Copyright 1996 Adam Twiss, Zeus Technology Ltd, http://www.zeustech.net/
Licensed to The Apache Software Foundation, http://www.apache.org/

Benchmarking  (be patient)
Completed 100 requests
Completed 200 requests
Completed 300 requests
Completed 400 requests
Completed 500 requests
Completed 600 requests
Completed 700 requests
Completed 800 requests
Completed 900 requests
Completed 1000 requests
Finished 1000 requests


Server Software:        Apache/2.2.16
Server Hostname:        
Server Port:            80

Document Path:          /
Document Length:        177 bytes

Concurrency Level:      1000
Time taken for tests:   0.656 seconds
Complete requests:      1000
Failed requests:        0
Write errors:           0
Total transferred:      453000 bytes
HTML transferred:       177000 bytes
Requests per second:    1523.82 [#/sec] (mean)
Time per request:       656.246 [ms] (mean)
Time per request:       0.656 [ms] (mean, across all concurrent requests)
Transfer rate:          674.11 [Kbytes/sec] received

Connection Times (ms)
              min  mean[+/-sd] median   max
Connect:       11   15   2.9     15      21
Processing:     6  158 209.8     32     631
Waiting:        5  158 209.9     32     631
Total:         20  174 210.7     53     646

Percentage of the requests served within a certain time (ms)
  50%     53
  66%    234
  75%    237
  80%    238
  90%    642
  95%    645
  98%    646
  99%    646
 100%    646 (longest request)

ab gives great data, and can generate a lot of load with minimal impact on
the launching system.

I eventually settled on:

ab -k -n 2000000 -c 50 http://<webserver>/

Use http-keepalive (for consistency with production) at 2 million requests making 50
simultaneously. These numbers were hitting a load of 8+ on my test server. Anything
averaging over 3.5 in production would be flagged to look at, but load testing is all
about pushing limits.

Getting macro numbers from ab. Run your tests a lot and look at overall numbers.

for i in {1..3}; do ab -n 1000 -c 1000 http://<webserver>/ >> test.txt; done

What is the average response time for request in the 95th percentile?

grep 95% test.txt | awk '{total = total + $2}END{print total}' 

4556 #<-- total number

I know I made 3 passes so my average 95th percentile response is:

echo "4556 / 3" | bc

1518 (ms)

This number is of limited value but adding your 95% through 100% and figuring out what
your response times are for 95th perctile and above can be useful.

Something like:

grep -A 3 95% test.txt | awk '{total = total + $2}END{print total}'

httping

I like using httping in the background or on another host as a consistent measure of my
response times.

httping -c 10 <webserver>

This makes 10 requests that look like:

connected to <webserver>:80, seq=0 time=0.97 ms

I like to flood the host with requests:

httping <webserver>  -f

Reference

http://linuxaria.com/article/put-your-site-under-siege?lang=en
http://www.cyberciti.biz/faq/linux-increase-the-maximum-number-of-open-files/
http://www.serverwatch.com/tutorials/article.php/3936526/Load-Testing-and-Benchmarking-With-Siege.htm
http://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.2/programs/ab.html
http://www.joedog.org/siege-home/
http://linux.die.net/man/1/siege

entry.title: debian graphite install
entry.content:
Preamble

Basic graphite install.

Installs


aptitude update
apt-get install -y python2.6 \
                   python-pip \
                   python-cairo \
                   python-django \
                   python-django-tagging \
                   libapache2-mod-wsgi \
                   python-twisted \
                   python-memcache \
                   python-pysqlite2 \
                   python-simplejson \
                   apache2 \
                   libapache2-mod-wsgi


pip install whisper
pip install carbon
pip install graphite-web


wget https://raw.github.com/tmm1/graphite/master/examples/example-graphite-vhost.conf \
     -O /etc/apache2/sites-available/graphite
nano /etc/apache2/sites-available/graphite

Example:


WSGISocketPrefix /var/run/apache2/wsgi

ServerName 
DocumentRoot "/opt/graphite/webapp"

#Allow our external dashboards, etc queries
Header set Access-Control-Allow-Origin "*"
Header set Access-Control-Allow-Methods "GET, OPTIONS"
Header set Access-Control-Allow-Headers "origin, authorization, accept"

# I've found that an equal number of processes & threads tends
# to show the best performance for Graphite (ymmv).
WSGIDaemonProcess graphite processes=5 threads=5 display-name='%{GROUP}' inactivity-timeout=120
WSGIProcessGroup graphite
WSGIApplicationGroup %{GLOBAL}
WSGIImportScript /opt/graphite/conf/graphite.wsgi process-group=graphite application-group=%{GLOBAL}

WSGIScriptAlias / /opt/graphite/conf/graphite.wsgi 

#require radius for any access

    AuthType Basic
    AuthName "Graphite auth"
    #SETUP YOUR AUTH HERE


Alias /content/ /opt/graphite/webapp/content/

    SetHandler None


# XXX In order for the django admin site media to work you
# must change @DJANGO_ROOT@ to be the path to your django
# installation, which is probably something like:
# /usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/django
Alias /media/ "@DJANGO_ROOT@/contrib/admin/media/"

    SetHandler None


# The graphite.wsgi file has to be accessible by apache. It won't
# be visible to clients because of the DocumentRoot though.

    Order deny,allow
    Allow from all



cd /opt/graphite/conf/
cp graphite.wsgi.example graphite.wsgi
cp carbon.conf.example carbon.conf
cp storage-schemas.conf.example storage-schemas.conf

Setup django db

cd /opt/graphite/webapp/graphite
python manage.py syncdb

Looks Like


Could not import graphite.local_settings, using defaults!
Could not import graphite.local_settings, using defaults!
Creating table account_profile
Creating table account_variable
Creating table account_view
Creating table account_window
Creating table account_mygraph
Creating table dashboard_dashboard_owners
Creating table dashboard_dashboard
Creating table events_event
Creating table auth_permission
Creating table auth_group_permissions
Creating table auth_group
Creating table auth_user_user_permissions
Creating table auth_user_groups
Creating table auth_user
Creating table auth_message
Creating table django_session
Creating table django_admin_log
Creating table django_content_type
Creating table tagging_tag
Creating table tagging_taggeditem

You just installed Django's auth system, which means you don't have any superusers defined.
Would you like to create one now? (yes/no): yes
Username (Leave blank to use 'root'): 
E-mail address: 
Error: That e-mail address is invalid.
E-mail address: root@localhost.com
Password: 
Password (again): 
Superuser created successfully.
Installing index for account.Variable model
Installing index for account.View model
Installing index for account.Window model
Installing index for account.MyGraph model
Installing index for dashboard.Dashboard_owners model
Installing index for auth.Permission model
Installing index for auth.Group_permissions model
Installing index for auth.User_user_permissions model
Installing index for auth.User_groups model
Installing index for auth.Message model
Installing index for admin.LogEntry model
Installing index for tagging.TaggedItem model
No fixtures found.

Enable site in Apache

a2dissite default
a2ensite graphite
sudo chmod -R a+w /opt/graphite/storage

Start things

/opt/graphite/bin/carbon-cache.py start
/etc/init.d/apache2 restart

Notes

Logs are located here: /opt/graphite/storage/log/webapp/

Reference

http://greenlegos.wordpress.com/2012/09/09/graphite-installation/

entry.title: bash then and now
entry.content:
Preamble

Very small doses of limited Bash wisdom. Changes in my every day
Bash behavior that are small but overtime I think make me more productive.

Going home

then:

cd ~

now:

cd

Erasing contents of a file

then:

cat /dev/null > file

now:

> file

Navigating relative dir's with cd

then:

cd ../../..

now:

uuu


alias u='cd .. && ls'
alias uu='cd ../.. && ls'
alias uuu='cd ../../.. && ls'
alias uuuu='cd ../../../.. && ls'
alias uuuuu='cd ../../../../..&& ls'
alias uuuuuu='cd ../../../../../.. && ls'
alias uuuuuuu='cd ../../../../../../.. && ls'
alias uuuuuuuu='cd ../../../../../../../.. && ls'

Switching between directories

then:

cd /get/me/there
cd /back/to/you
cd /get/me/there

now:

cd /get/me/there
cd /back/to/you
cd -

env | grep OLDPWD
OLDPWD=/back/to/you

Realizing I'm not ready to run a command yet

then:

~#get#### stuff#### done <realizing now ready>
<grab mouse, highlight, copy>
<do pre-get#### stuff#### done>
<grab mouse, click, paste>

now:

~#get#### stuff#### done <realizing now ready>
<cntrl+a enter ':'>
~#:get#### stuff#### done
-bash: :get#### stuff#### done: command not found
<ignore cmd not found>
<do pre-get#### stuff#### done>
<up arrow to cmd>
<cntrl+a delete ':' and enter>

No mouse required.

Getting my boxes IP

then:

ifconfig

<visually search for ip>

now:

#ips
eth0 10.0.0.1/16

bash aliases

alias ips='ip brief'


#IP SHORTCUTS
ip () {
    case "$1" in
        'br')
            ip brief
            ;;
        'brief')
            sudo ip addr show | grep inet | grep -v 127 | awk '{print $7, $2}' | grep -v '::'
            ;;
        'alias')
            echo $"Usage: ip 
                         [alias|br|brief]"
            ;;
        *)
        sudo /bin/ip $@
    esac
}

Grepping files

then:

 cat /etc/services | grep telnet

now:

grep telnet /etc/services

Exiting (hey...saves a few keystrokes)

then:

#exit

now:

#e

alias EXIT="exit"
alias e='exit'

New dir trees

Then:

mkdir /one
mkdir /one/two
mkdir /one/two/three

Now:

mkdir -p one/two/three

Show routing table

Then:

route -n

Now:

ip route show

entry.title: puppet tagged runs
entry.content:
Preamble

Updating only relevant Puppet configurations sections

As a puppet installation grows the list of used modules can get large. Especially,
if you are pulling from https://forge.puppetlabs.com/. Testing in its final stages
is usually done via puppet to ensure repeatability. The test runs get longer and longer.

A full catalog run with no expected changes



sudo /usr/bin/puppet agent --test --logdest syslog





Info: Retrieving plugin

Info: Caching catalog for 

Info: Applying configuration version '1371116293'

Finished catalog run in 16.35 seconds

If you need to rerun this every time you are tweaking a config file 16 seconds can be a long time.

If you are only updating a module called 'dhcp' you can do:



sudo /usr/bin/puppet agent --test --logdest syslog --tags dhcp





Info: Retrieving plugin

Info: Caching catalog for 

Info: Applying configuration version '1371116293'

Finished catalog run in 8.46 seconds

This cut the time in half. Doing tagged runs still compiles the entire update. That is to
say, Puppet still runs in the usual way figuring out dependencies and such, but when it
comes to applicable changes it only looks for changes at the specified tagged resources.

If you put '--debug' into the update command you will see a lot of:

Debug: /Stage[main]/Mcollective::Client/Package[mcollective-client]: Not tagged with dhcp

This resource does not match the tag specified, and as such will not be updated.

Tags can be explicit or implicit. That is to say you can assign them and there are some
that are assigned by design. All class/module names are also tags.

This automatically creates a tag called 'hiera':



class hiera {

}

Resource types are generally given a tag as well.

To update file resources only:



sudo /usr/bin/puppet agent --test --logdest syslog --tags file





Info: Retrieving plugin

Info: Caching catalog for 

Info: Applying configuration version '1371116293'

Finished catalog run in 12.39 seconds

Reference:

http://docs.puppetlabs.com/puppet/2.7/reference/lang_tags.html

entry.title: mysql full partition
entry.content:
Preamble

Ever go to start mysql and there is not enough disk space. We need to get
MySQL past disk full warnings.

I tried to:

/etc/init.d/mysql start

And saw:

/etc/init.d/mysql: ERROR: The partition with /var/lib/mysql is too full! ... failed!

Usage

df -Th


Filesystem    Type    Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda1     ext3    7.6G  7.5G     0 100% /
tmpfs        tmpfs    249M     0  249M   0% /lib/init/rw
udev         tmpfs    244M  172K  244M   1% /dev
tmpfs        tmpfs    249M     0  249M   0% /dev/shm
overflow     tmpfs    1.0M  1.0M     0 100% /tmp
.host:/trunk
            vmhgfs    233G  127G  107G  55% /www/repo

So I want to take a look at my top disk hogs:

du -a | sort -nr | head


7920858 .
5334316 ./var
3137636 ./var/log
1220480 ./usr
1079740 ./var/cache
1073176 ./var/cache/apt
1040900 ./var/cache/apt/archives
983716  ./var/lib
888564  ./var/log/kern.log
852852  ./var/log/messages

Looks like /var/log and apt cache are out of control.

many rm -fR's later...


/dev/sda1     ext3    7.6G  5.5G  1.7G  77% /
tmpfs        tmpfs    249M     0  249M   0% /lib/init/rw
udev         tmpfs    244M  172K  244M   1% /dev
tmpfs        tmpfs    249M     0  249M   0% /dev/shm
overflow     tmpfs    1.0M  1.0M     0 100% /tmp

Let's try to start:

/etc/init.d/mysql start


Starting MySQL (Percona Server) database server: mysqld ..
Checking for corrupt, not cleanly closed and upgrade needing tables..
ERROR 1030 (HY000) at line 2: Got error 28 from storage engine

StackOverflow tells us this still means 'not enough disk space'.

Obviously we need to clean up /tmp


/dev/sda1     ext3    7.6G  5.5G  1.7G  77% /
tmpfs        tmpfs    249M     0  249M   0% /lib/init/rw
udev         tmpfs    244M  172K  244M   1% /dev
tmpfs        tmpfs    249M     0  249M   0% /dev/shm
overflow     tmpfs    1.0M  1.0M     0 100% /tmp

rm -fR /tmp/*


/dev/sda1     ext3    7.6G  5.5G  1.7G  77% /
tmpfs        tmpfs    249M     0  249M   0% /lib/init/rw
udev         tmpfs    244M  172K  244M   1% /dev
tmpfs        tmpfs    249M     0  249M   0% /dev/shm
overflow     tmpfs    1.0M     0  1.0M   0% /tmp

Ta-da

/etc/init.d/mysql start

Starting MySQL (Percona Server) database server: mysqld already running.

Notes

aptitude install tmpreaper

Reference:

http://packages.debian.org/search?keywords=tmpreaper
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/10631387/1030-got-error-28-from-storage-engine
http://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/ta-da

entry.title: code test beginnings
entry.content:
Preamble

Testing your code as you go. Writing 'production' code is really a set of practices
rather than a specific coding ability. Everyone breaks things. When something breaks
we will all fix it. The trick is to always know when something breaks.

How I currently see the world:

  • Write code
  • Run unit tests

It continues on ...

  • Run integration tests
  • Run QA/Functionality testing
  • Test at scale
  • Test monitoring
  • Test Security

There are a lot of ways to break down this basic idea. Some much better than this. But I
feel like if you can visualize these 6 you are going in the right direction.

Write Code

def what():
    return '123'

if __name__ == '__main__':
    print what()

This is my big application. When I run it I see.


   123

Unit Testing - Testing Small Things

My idea of unittesting is if every function and class takes care of itself the entire
codebase is better off.

Doctest is a way to write small tests into the docstring. Some people do not consider this
to be valid unittesting. I think it's a good start.

def what():
    """
    >>> what()
    '123'
    """
    return '123'

if __name__ == '__main__':
    what()

'python -m doctest t.py' returns nothing.

But:

'python -m doctest t.py  -v' returns useful output:


Trying:
    what()
Expecting:
    '123'
ok
1 items had no tests:
    t
1items passed all tests:
   1 tests in t.what
1 tests in 2 items.
1 passed and 0 failed.
Test passed.

Everything passed. In the most basic sense the code in the doctstring returned expected results.

What if my function code changes and the doctest becomes invalid?

Change it to:

def what():
    """
    >>> what()
    '1234'
    """
    return '123'

Now we see:


:~$ python -m doctest t.py -v
Trying:
    what()
Expecting:
    '1234'
**********************************************************************
File "t.py", line 3, in t.what
Failed example:
    what()
Expected:
    '1234'
Got:
    '123'
1 items had no tests:
    t
**********************************************************************
1 items had failures:
   1 of   1 in t.what
1 tests in 2 items.
0 passed and 1 failed.
***Test Failed*** 1 failures.
:~$

You can run this as a pre-commit hook and you will never break this function without knowing.

mkdir repo
cd repo
git init
nano pre-commit

Contents:

#!/bin/bash
python -m doctest *

cd .git/hooks/
ln -s ../../pre-commit pre-commit
cd ../..
git add -A
git commit -m 'test'

We see our failure message:


**********************************************************************
File "t.py", line 5, in t.what
Failed example:
    what()
Expected:
    '1234'
Got:
    '123'
**********************************************************************
1 items had failures:
   1 of   1 in t.what
***Test Failed*** 1 failures.

We can fix it and make good.

One step up from '-m doctest'

I like to use nose to run my tests. It can handle tests written in multiple styles
and most code review tools integrate with it.

aptitude install python-nose

nano pre-commit

Contents:

#!/bin/bash
nosetests --all-modules --traverse-namespace --with-doctest --exe

This output is more readable as your tests grow:


:~/no$ git commit -m 'nose'
F
======================================================================
FAIL: Doctest: t.what
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/doctest.py", line 2166, in runTest
    raise self.failureException(self.format_failure(new.getvalue()))
AssertionError: Failed doctest test for t.what
  File "/Users//no/t.py", line 3, in what

----------------------------------------------------------------------
File "/Users//no/t.py", line 5, in t.what
Failed example:
    what()
Expected:
    '1234'
Got:
    '123'


----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.006s

FAILED (failures=1)

Separating your test from your function

Ok up until now we have been validating our docstring and that is valuable. But
it is also cluttered and can be unwieldy. Writing a basic test function. I also
added '-v' to the nosetest hook.

This function should literally start with 'test'


def test_what():
    assert what() == '123'

def what():
    return '123'

if __name__ == '__main__':
    print what()

Looks like


t.test_what ... ok

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.002s

OK

Thus the groundlevel for self testing code.

Reference

http://git-scm.com/book/en/Customizing-Git-Git-Hooks
https://nose.readthedocs.org/en/latest/

entry.title: IOS DDOS detection
entry.content:
Preamble

Basic DDOS detection and defense on IOS FW

Aliases

alias exec fstats show policy-firewall stats global
alias exec zstats show policy-firewall stats zone

IOS Zone Based FW Config

Per Zone

conf t
parameter-map type inspect-zone ddos-detection
 !this is per _your_ hosts not source hosts
 tcp syn-flood rate per-destination <global_syn_flood_rate_limit_per_host>>
 alert on
 threat-detection basic-threat
 !these alert limits should be changed to something reasonable for your environment
 threat-detection rate syn-attack average-time-frame 600 average-threshold 100 burst-threshold 100
 threat-detection rate inspect-drop average-time-frame 600 average-threshold 100 burst-threshold 100
 threat-detection rate fw-drop average-time-frame 600 average-threshold 100 burst-threshold 100

 !apply pmap to a zone
 zone security ZONE_INTERNET
  protection ddos-detection

Global

parameter-map type inspect global
 !if you want a global sessions threshold
 session total 3000000
 !syn flood limit before going to syn cookies globally
 per-box tcp syn-flood limit 10000
 alert on
 !aging out sessions aggressively to help prevent table blocking
 per-box aggressive-aging high percent 85 low percent 65
 per-box max-incomplete 100000 aggressive-aging high percent 85 low percent 60

Show Zone Stats

asr01#zstats
Zone: ZONE_INTERNET
Parameter-map: ddos-detection
TCP SYN packet conform limit: 0
TCP SYN packet exceed limit: 0
Threat Detection Statistics:
                        Average(eps)    Current(eps)    Threats Total Events
10-min SYN attack:      0               0               0               0
10-min FW inspect:      0               0               0               0
10-min FW policy:       0               0               0               0

Show Global Stats

Global (Per-Box) statistics
Total Session Aggressive Aging Period Off, Event Count: 0

         Half Open  
Protocol Session Cnt     Exceed
-------- -----------     ------
All      0               0
UDP      0               0
ICMP     0               0
TCP      0               0

Logging Shows

6052: Jun 6 01:41:28.864: %IOSXE-4-PLATFORM: F0:
cpp_cp: QFP:00 Thread:007 TS:00000106170032787836
%THREAT_DEFENSE-4-TD_ERM_AVERAGE_THREAT:
Event Rate Monitoring: Average threat identified.
Interface [<your_interface>], event type [firewall inspection drops],
average [101] configured threshold [100]
TCP Syn Flood Half Open Count: 1, Exceed: 0
Half Open Aggressive Aging Period Off, Event Count: 0

entry.title: IOS history size
entry.content:
Preamble

Changing your session buffer for better command navigation.

Per VTY

History buffer size in IOS is weird. It's per VTY. So you have to
change it for all virtual terminals in service.

Your VTY configuration probably looks something like:

line vty 0 4
 transport input ssh
 escape-character 3
line vty 5 15
 transport input ssh

Change settings for VTY's

line vty 0 15
 !256 is the max
 history size 256

Alias

alias exec hg show history | include

Looks like:

<your_device>#hg conf
    hg conf

entry.title: mib basics
entry.content:
Preamble

SNMP is nice but MIB translations can be a pain. It can also be necessary for figuring
out what certain numbers mean in the MIB tree.

Where does SNMP look for MIBs?

net-snmp-config --default-mibdirs

Make user specific directory (needs to be in above path)

mkdir -p /home/<me>/.snmp/mibs

Grab MIB data from Cisco (or wherever)

Example MIB for Firewall info:

ftp://ftp.cisco.com/pub/mibs/v2/CISCO-UNIFIED-FIREWALL-MIB.my

Copy to:

/home/<me>/.snmp/mibs/CISCO-UNIFIED-FIREWALL-MIB

Enable MIB

Find out where SNMP is looking for configuration parameters:

net-snmp-config --snmpconfpath

Edit your user snmp configuration (if path is included in above command)

n /home/<me>/.snmp/snmp.conf
mib +CISCO-UNIFIED-FIREWALL-MIB

Try walking the top OID tree

snmpwalk -v2c <community> <remote_device_ip>

MIB search path: /home/<me>/.snmp/mibs:/usr/share/mibs/site:/usr/share/snmp/mibs:/usr/share/mibs/iana:/usr/share/mibs/ietf:/usr/share/mibs/netsnmp
Cannot find module (CISCO-SMI): At line 37 in /home/<me>/.snmp/mibs/CISCO-UNIFIED-FIREWALL-MIB
Cannot find module (CISCO-FIREWALL-TC): At line 46 in /home/<me>/.snmp/mibs/CISCO-UNIFIED-FIREWALL-MIB

What's with? 'Cannot find module'

This means there are MIB dependencies. You can probably retrieve these from ftp://ftp.cisco.com/pub/mibs/v2.

/home/<me>/.snmp/mibs/CISCO-SMI
/home/<me>/.snmp/mibs/CISCO-FIREWALL-TC

Enable these MIBs as well.

Walk the tree and enjoy MIB goodness

<me>@<box>:~# snmpwalk -v2c -c <community> <remote_device_ip> >> snmpinfo.txt

Show me the basic SNMP catagories

You can use cut to see the basics as a full snmpwalk can be a lot of data.

cut -d: -f 1 snmpinfo.txt | uniq | sort

SNMPv2-MIB
Technical Support
Copyright (c) 1986-2013 by Cisco Systems, Inc.
SNMPv2-MIB
DISMAN-EVENT-MIB
SNMPv2-MIB
IF-MIB
RFC1213-MIB
IP-MIB
IP-FORWARD-MIB
IP-MIB
TCP-MIB
UDP-MIB
SNMPv2-SMI
SNMPv2-MIB
SNMPv2-SMI

You can check out DISMAN-EVENT-MIB using grep. If you find a MIB you can use SNMPGET next time.

Retrieve Uptime Using MIB's and SNMPGET

<me>@<box>:~# snmpget -v2c -c <community> <remote_device_ip> DISMAN-EVENT-MIB::sysUpTimeInstance

DISMAN-EVENT-MIB::sysUpTimeInstance = Timeticks: (151866995) 17 days, 13:51:09.95

Reference:

ftp://ftp.cisco.com/pub/mibs/v2/CISCO-SMI.my
ftp://ftp.cisco.com/pub/mibs/v2/CISCO-FIREWALL-TC.my
http://www.cisco.com/public/sw-center/netmgmt/cmtk/mibs.shtml
http://www.net-snmp.org/wiki/index.php/TUT:Using_and_loading_MIBS
http://wiki.debian.org/SNMP
http://www.cisco.com/en/US/docs/routers/asr1000/mib/guide/asr1mib2.html#wp1034752
http://www.cisco.com/en/US/docs/routers/asr1000/mib/guide/asr1mib2.html#wp1024774
ftp://ftp.cisco.com/pub/mibs/v2
ftp://ftp.cisco.com/pub/mibs/contrib

entry.title: email log alerts
entry.content:
Preamble

Getting emails based on strings that appear in a log file.

Heavy duty logging platforms are nice but sometimes you have
to make do with the basics.

Install

aptitude install log2mail rsyslog

Rsyslog configuration

#one dir/file per receiver IP
$template FILENAME,"/var/log/%fromhost-ip%/syslog.log"
*.* ?FILENAME

Editing log2mail config

We want to match a text string in our log file.

Edit: /etc/log2mail/config/default


file = /var/log//syslog.log
    pattern = ""
    mailto = @

The default alert email

Once your string is matched you should get an email:

Hello!

We have matched your pattern "<YOUR_TEXT_PATTERN>" in "<YOUR_LOG_FILE>" 5 times:

<YOUR LOG FILE MATCHES>

Yours,
log2mail.

To edit this default template you need to change: /etc/log2mail/mail

Reference

http://people.debian.org/~enrico/log2mail/

entry.title: adding a logfile to splunk
entry.content:
Getting standard logfiles into Splunk

inputs.conf

[monitor:///var/log/my/file.log]
disabled = false
followTail = 0
host = <HOST>
host_segment = 4
sourcetype = <SOURCE_TYPE_NAME>

Notes

You should add it to to logrotate, Splunk will "tail" it correctly

entry.title: convert mp3 for asterisk
entry.content:
Preamble

I want to use mp3's for hold music, and for fun.

Convert all mp3's in a directory for use with asterisk.

#!/bin/bash
for f in `ls *.mp3` ; do FILE=$(basename $f .mp3) ; \
ffmpeg -i $FILE.mp3 -ar 8000 -ac 1 -ab 64 $FILE.wav -ar 8000 \
-ac 1 -ab 64 -f mulaw $FILE.pcm -map 0:0 -map 0:0 ; done

Asterisk Configuration

extensions.conf

; Answer required as Music On Hold does not answer the call
exten => 2112,1,Answer
exten => 2112,2,MusicOnHold(rush)

I used this to play 2112 whenever you dial extension 2112.

entry.title: iptables flush all rules
entry.content:
Handy for testing. Flush iptables.

#!/bin/bash
iptables -F
iptables -X
iptables -t nat -F
iptables -t nat -X
iptables -t mangle -F
iptables -t mangle -X
iptables -P INPUT ACCEPT
iptables -P FORWARD ACCEPT
iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT
iptables -L -n -v

entry.title: simple iptables uses
entry.content:
Allow ssh from only 192 network (presumably your internal)

iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp -s 192.168.0.0/16 --dport 22 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -o eth0 -p tcp --sport 22 -m state --state ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT

Allow SCCP (VOIP Protocol)

iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 1720 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -o eth0 -p tcp --sport 1720 -m state --state ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT

Allow h.323 (VOIP)

iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 2000 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -o eth0 -p tcp --sport 2000 -m state --state ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT

Allow ICMP

iptables -A OUTPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-reply -j ACCEPT

Allow DNS queries, etc

iptables -A OUTPUT -p udp -o eth0 --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p udp -i eth0 --sport 53 -j ACCEPT

Allow mail sending

iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 25 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -o eth0 -p tcp --sport 25 -m state --state ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT

Allowing SSH and HTTP and self sourced traffic

iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 22 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -o eth0 -p tcp --sport 22 -m state --state ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 80 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -o eth0 -p tcp --sport 80 -m state --state ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
iptables -P INPUT DROP
iptables -P FORWARD DROP
iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT

entry.title: query puppetdb facts from cli
entry.content:
Preamble

A need to get host facts when they are not up. PuppetDB has cached them, and
I like the CLI.

This doesn't work like you think it does

puppet facts find <myhost.com>

This always returns the facts for the puppetmaster itself.

Setting Terminus

If you want to query the puppetdb store directly set the terminus.

puppet facts find <myhost.com> --terminus puppetdb

This returns the 'right stuff'. Beware it could be old because it's last cached values.

Readability Counts

puppet facts find <myhost.com> --terminus puppetdb | python -m json.tool

Reference:

http://docs.puppetlabs.com/puppetdb/latest/api/query/v2/fact-names.html
http://docs.python.org/2/library/json.html

entry.title: using snort on pcap
entry.content:
Preamble

Grabbing tcpdump output during a crisis can be hard to remember. Ideally, snort
is running as as service inline or at least continually. Sometimes things happen
outside of Snort's purview or you are testing what Snort picks ups.

Install tools

aptitude install snort snort-rules

Capture your traffic

sudo tcpdump -w test

Generate snort report

snort -r test -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -l .

View Alerts

less alert 

[**] [1:485:4] ICMP Destination Unreachable Communication Administratively Prohibited [**]
[Classification: Misc activity] [Priority: 3] 
05/19-01:23:00.201765 74.76.148.114 -> <my_ext_ip>
ICMP TTL:241 TOS:0x0 ID:32480 IpLen:20 DgmLen:56
Type:3  Code:13  DESTINATION UNREACHABLE: ADMINISTRATIVELY PROHIBITED,
PACKET FILTERED
** ORIGINAL DATAGRAM DUMP:
<my_ext_ip>:80 -> 74.76.148.114:25657
TCP TTL:47 TOS:0x0 ID:22893 IpLen:20 DgmLen:44
Seq: 0x654B5258
** END OF DUMP

You can edit your snort.conf for what kinds of signatures to look for. ICMP can be a good indicator
of shenanigans but is also very noisy.

entry.title: bash boiler plate troubleshooting
entry.content:
Preamble

I have a bash script that is being called multiple times instead of once.
I need to track down where it is being called from.

Boiler plate that is nice for troubleshooting

Snippet


function trouble {
    echo "--------------------"
    date
    echo "whoami: $(whoami)"
    echo "userid: $(id -u)"
    echo "My pid is $$"
    echo "working dir: $(pwd)"
    echo "Called as: $0"
    echo "Arguments: $@"
    echo "user_pstree_begin"
    pstree $(whoami) -l
    echo "user_pstree_end"
    echo "pid_pstree_begin"
    pstree -np $$
    echo "pid_pstree_end"
    echo "--------------------"
}

set -x
epochtime=$(date +%s)
file="/tmp/debug-$epochtime.log"
logger "Creating: $file"
trouble > $file

Looks like


@:~# cat /tmp/debug-1369940847.log 
--------------------
Thu May 30 12:07:27 PDT 2013
whoami: 
userid: 0
My pid is 19891
ps_tree_begin
test.sh(19891)---pstree(19896)
ps_tree_end
working dir: /home/
Called as: ./test.sh
Arguments: 
--------------------
@:~#

Syslog shows:

<host> <me>: Creating: /tmp/debug-1369940847.log

This is especially helpful for cron jobs or background scripts.

entry.title: watching linux filesystem events
entry.content:
Preamble

When you need to watch file system events efficiently.

Install user land tools:

aptitude install inotify-tools

Waiting for a delete

This waits for a file to be deleted and then echo's:

#!/bin/bash
inotifywait -e delete_self ./hi/yes  && echo "gone"

Waiting for a create

This watches for files created in a directory and then echo's. This
is more appropriately done with the -m option in real life:

#!/bin/bash
while true; do

inotifywait -e create ./hi  && echo "yo"
done

Watching for changes in /home


#!/bin/bash
while true; do
    touch /var/log/changes
    inotifywait -r -e create "/home" |
    while read filename eventlist eventfile
    do
        echo "see new $eventfile"
        echo "creation $eventfile" >> /var/log/changes
    done
done

Reference:

http://www.infoq.com/articles/inotify-linux-file-system-event-monitoring
http://pyinotify.sourceforge.net/
http://www.ibm.com/developerworks/library/l-inotify/
http://blog.lagentz.com/general/automate-your-shell-scripts-using-inotify-and-inotifywait/
http://linux.die.net/man/1/inotifywait

entry.title: launching a syn flood test
entry.content:
Preamble

Launching a SYN flood.

Everyone know DDOS attacks happen and of these SYN floods may be the simplest to organize for
attackers. As a defender you don't want the first time you see this kind of traffic to
be when you are under attack.

Launching a SYN attack against yourself.

  1. You can learn some tools of the trade
  2. You can test the weakness of your services
  3. You can mitigate those weaknesses

Picking a tool

A tool that is simple to use: https://github.com/merces/t50

git clone https://github.com/merces/t50.git
cd t50/
make
./t50 --help

Now you have a compiled attack tool and the help is pretty large.

Launching a SYN Flood

./t50 <DEST_IP> --flood -S --turbo

Looks like

Start:

entering in flood mode...
activating turbo...
hit CTRL+C to break.

Finish: 

T50 5.4.1 successfully launched on May 28th 2013 13:09:24

n the destination host:

  • Traffic:

    tx eth0: 1168 b/s rx eth0: 528 b/s
    tx eth0: 1056 b/s rx eth0: 9160 b/s
    tx eth0: 8616 b/s rx eth0: 528 b/s
    tx eth0: 4944 b/s rx eth0: 528 b/s

        *syn flood starts**

    tx eth0: 10 Mb/s rx eth0: 12 Mb/s
    tx eth0: 36 Mb/s rx eth0: 43 Mb/s
    tx eth0: 38 Mb/s rx eth0: 46 Mb/s
    tx eth0: 39 Mb/s rx eth0: 47 Mb/s
    tx eth0: 39 Mb/s rx eth0: 47 Mb/s
  • Logging (NOTE: SYN Cookies are enabled)

    possible SYN flooding on port 5666. Sending cookies.
    possible SYN flooding on port 5666. Sending cookies.
  • Connection Table

    while true; do netstat -n -p TCP tcp | grep  SYN_RECV | wc -l >> /tmp/syn.log; sleep 2; done

    0
    5
    6
    3
    2
    ...
    **syn flood**
    ...
    142
    140
    144
    143
    142
    142
    143
    142
    141
    141
    140
    137
    138
    140
    142
    142
    142
    145
    144

Effect:

  • Massive lag in responsiveness for CLI commands
  • Simple web server with (python -m SimpleHTTPServer) crashed
  • Top shows ksoftirqd/0 pegging CPU

Guidance on size of flood:

./t50 <target> --threshold 10000 -S #4Mbps
./t50 <target> --threshold 20000 -S #8Mbps
./t50 <target> --threshold 40000 -S #16Mbps

#more or less consisten 4Mbps flood
for i in {1..100}; do ./t50 <target> --threshold 10000 -S; sleep 3; done

Reference:

http://antmeetspenguin.blogspot.com/2011/01/detecting-and-preventing-syn-flood.html
http://www.sans.org/security-resources/idfaq/syn_flood.php
http://www.cs.wm.edu/~hnw/paper/attack.pdf
http://serverfault.com/questions/294209/possible-syn-flooding-in-log-despite-low-number-of-syn-recv-connections
http://pc-freak.net/blog/how-to-check-if-your-linux-webserver-is-under-a-dos-attack/
https://github.com/merces/t50
http://www.gossamer-threads.com/lists/fulldisc/full-disclosure/77770?do=post_view_threaded
http://www.cisco.com/web/about/ac123/ac147/archived_issues/ipj_9-4/syn_flooding_attacks.html
http://h4xors.wordpress.com/tag/t50/

entry.title: cisco lights out failsafe
entry.content:
Preamble

If you are remote and are at risk of locking yourself out. For Cisco or IOS based devices
your best bet is to schedule a reload at the beginning of your maintenance. This mitigates
the possible outage. Better 30 minutes than 30 hours, if you are using JunOS they have some
nice "fail back to my previous config" logic that Cisco hasn't figured out yet. Yes, this will
obviously cause an outage, but in a true 'lights out' remote scenario this may be the best
you can do.

Walk through:

It's 2 AM and I'm up for maintenace

myrtr#reload in 30
System configuration has been modified. Save? [yes/no]: no
Reload scheduled for 13:20:23 PDT Wed May 29 2013 (in 30 minutes) by <me> on vty0 (<vty_ip>)
Reload reason: Reload Command
Proceed with reload? [confirm]
myrtr#

*** Making changes ***

*** How long until a reset? ***

myrtr#show reload
Reload scheduled for 13:20:24 PDT Wed May 29 2013 (in 12 minutes) by <me> on vty0 (<vty_ip>)
Reload reason: Reload Command

*** test everything is good -- ok clear scheduled reload ***

myrtr#reload cancel
***
*** --- SHUTDOWN ABORTED ---
***

entry.title: beginning bgp configuration
entry.content:
Preamble

Getting going with BGP

Configuring Basic BGP (full table)

This configuration is Cisco based but JunOS isn't too far of a stretch in my experience.

First off: you need your ASN. You need your address block. Your address block must be at least /24.

A general rule of thumb for awhile has been 2 GB of memory per full BGP table. Sometimes more is
required and sometimes significantly less. The table is still growing and 2 GB is my personal
baseline required.

Aliases: you will learn to like them

alias exec sumbgp6 show bgp ipv6 unicast summary
alias exec sumbgp show ip bgp summary
alias exec sbgp show run | section bgp

Basic BGP stanza


config t
ip bgp-community new-format
!insert your ASN
router bgp 
 !your router id should be set explicitly
 bgp router-id 
 no bgp fast-external-fallover
 !notify syslog of bgp changes
 bgp log-neighbor-changes
 bgp graceful-restart restart-time 120
 bgp graceful-restart stalepath-time 360
 bgp graceful-restart
 neighbor  remote-as 
 neighbor  description 
 neighbor  version 4
 neighbor  activate

Our First BGP Summary


RTRME(config-router)####do sumbgp
BGP router identifier , local AS number 
BGP table version is 464187, main routing table version 464187
446501 network entries using 66082148 bytes of memory
446501 path entries using 28576064 bytes of memory
73561/73558 BGP path/bestpath attribute entries using 9415808 bytes of memory
69308 BGP AS-PATH entries using 2555070 bytes of memory
68 BGP community entries using 1904 bytes of memory
0 BGP route-map cache entries using 0 bytes of memory
0 BGP filter-list cache entries using 0 bytes of memory
BGP using 106630994 total bytes of memory
BGP activity 460823/1679 prefixes, 461759/2621 paths, scan interval 60 secs

Neighbor        V           AS MsgRcvd MsgSent   TblVer  InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
  4         2914   79993      80   464166    0    0 01:11:19   446501

BGP: Why you no work?


RTRME(config-router)####do sh ip bgp neighbor  advertised-routes

Total number of prefixes 0

To Advertise it: We need to know where it is


RTRME(config)####sh ip bgp 
% Network not in table

RTRME(config)####ip route   null 0 200

RTRME(config)####do sh ip bgp 
BGP routing table entry for , version 464822
Paths: (1 available, best ####1, table default)
Multipath: eBGP
  Not advertised to any peer
  Refresh Epoch 1
  Local
    0.0.0.0 from 0.0.0.0 ()
      Origin IGP, metric 0, localpref 100, weight 32768, valid, sourced, local, best
RTRME(config)####

Resetting BGP

This is very aggressive and will reset your peering status. Lookup soft in/out if
you are alreadying using BGP in production.



RTRME####clear ip bgp *
